coredata.dc:title: Preventing fomite transmission using antiviral materials: Perspectives on food packaging after COVID-19 pandemic
coredata.dc:creator: Priyadarshi, Ruchir
Purohit, Shiv Dutt
Ghosh, Tabli
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: The SARS-CoV-2 virus, which caused the recent COVID-19 pandemic, is highly contagious due to its exponential transmission rate, unlike other viruses in the coronavirus family. In addition to the usual transmission mode via respiratory droplets, fomite-mediated transmission has been another major cause of its high spreadability. Evidence shows that COVID-19 is transmitted through food packaging, which is believed to act as a fomite for the virus. Various reports also state that SARS-CoV-2 persists for at least three days on the plastic surfaces that form the basis of food packaging. This review highlights the importance of antiviral materials in food packaging to inhibit the fomite-based transmission of viruses. Much research has already been conducted on active antibacterial/antifungal packaging materials. Most constituent materials of antibacterial packaging, such as polymers, fillers, and active agents, possess antiviral properties and can be used to manufacture antiviral packaging materials. This review discusses these antiviral materials and focuses on their mechanism of action, current use, and recent developments in antiviral food packaging during the COVID-19 pandemic. Also, the research prospects of these materials in the post-pandemic period have been discussed.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Various types of viral particles according to their structure: (a) helical, (b) icosahedral, (c) spherical, and (d) complex.

The basic structure of SARS-CoV-2 showing its constituent proteins acting as therapeutic targets for potential antiviral agents: (a) Surface structure of coronavirus, (b) Cross section structure showing internal components of the virus. Created with BioRender.com.

Illustration of the infection mechanism of SARS-CoV-2. The S-protein on the virus particle interacts with the ACE-2 receptor on the host cell membrane, which initiates the entry of the virus into the host cell. Once the virus particle invades the host cell, the virus genome is released, translating and giving rise to more virus particles (viral replication) using host ribosomal machinery. The produced viruses spread and infect other cells, resulting in a cascade of immune reactions, destroying infected host cells by the immune system. Created with BioRender.com.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the reactive blade coating RC process of AgNP-based thin film on the glass. (A) Along one side of the blade, reducer ink droplets containing a 30% v/v solution of oleylamine in toluene (TOL) were coated on the glass substrate. (B) On top of the initial reducing layer, precursor ink containing 30 mg/mL silver nitrate dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was coated. The coated substrate was then heated at 80 °C for 1 h, followed by 3 h at 120 °C, producing AgNPs coating. Copyright (2022) American Chemical Society.

ce:figure.ce:source: Adopted with permission from Abulikemu et al. (2022).

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 6

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The application of (A) hydrophilic graphene oxide (GO) and (B) hydrophobic graphene (G) against SARS-CoV-2. The pre-incubation of the virus with GO almost completely inhibits the infectivity due to the hydrophilicity of GO, which binds and entraps suspended SARS-CoV-2 viral particles. Further, virus filtration by G- or GO-functionalized cotton or PU substantially eliminated the SARS-CoV-2 infection and spread. Copyright (2021) Elsevier.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Inhibition of influenza by polyvalent SA-containing polymers as demonstrated by the Hemagglutination Inhibition assay. SA side chains bind to HA spikes on influenza’s surface, rendering the virus unable to bind to the host cells, preventing the infection. Copyright (2020) American Chemical Society.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The SARS-CoV-2 virus, which caused the recent COVID-19 pandemic, is highly contagious due to its exponential transmission rate, unlike other viruses in the coronavirus family. In addition to the usual transmission mode via respiratory droplets, fomite-mediated transmission has been another major cause of its high spreadability. Evidence shows that COVID-19 is transmitted through food packaging, which is believed to act as a fomite for the virus. Various reports also state that SARS-CoV-2 persists for at least three days on the plastic surfaces that form the basis of food packaging. This review highlights the importance of antiviral materials in food packaging to inhibit the fomite-based transmission of viruses. Much research has already been conducted on active antibacterial/antifungal packaging materials. Most constituent materials of antibacterial packaging, such as polymers, fillers, and active agents, possess antiviral properties and can be used to manufacture antiviral packaging materials. This review discusses these antiviral materials and focuses on their mechanism of action, current use, and recent developments in antiviral food packaging during the COVID-19 pandemic. Also, the research prospects of these materials in the post-pandemic period have been discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The recent coronavirus pandemic, COVID-19, caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus strain, was the most threatening catastrophic event this generation of humanity witnessed. Since the outbreak in late 2019, more than 500 million people have been infected, and more than 6 million have died, according to the World Health Organization (WHO) (Shahdeo, Chauhan, et al., 2022; World Health Organization, 2022). The virulence severity of SARS-CoV-2 is due to the unusually high transmission rate of this infectious agent within the human population (Banerjee et al., 2020). In addition, it spreads from asymptomatic infected people to healthy people in the early stages, making its detection and control difficult for the medical and research communities (Qiu et al., 2021).

Nearly 100 countries worldwide have had to enforce lockdowns, social distancing, and quarantine rules, which led to a sudden increase in online product delivery systems to give people access to necessities (Priyadarshi et al., 2022). This resulted in a drastic surge in online food orders, including raw and fresh fruits and vegetables, processed and packaged home meal replacements, and ready-to-eat foods (Gavilan et al., 2021). Meanwhile, ongoing research on this new virus shows it also spreads through routes other than direct person-to-person contact, including fomite-mediated transmission (Galbadage et al., 2020; Ji et al., 2021). Fomites, defined as surfaces capable of carrying and transmitting viruses, were soon discovered as the most common and highly responsible route for spreading this contagious disease. The surfaces of food packages delivered to consumers are also suspected of playing a role. Several reports have been published indicating the role of delivery food and packaging as carriers of the coronavirus (Castrica et al., 2021). While European Union highlighted the possibility of viral transmission via food packages, the first report on packaging material-mediated cross-contamination was revealed in China, where meat packages of Brazilian origin were found contaminated with coronavirus (Jyoti & Bhattacharya, 2021; Pang et al., 2020). Besides, the virus's average survival time was 72 h on plastic surfaces and 24 h on cardboard surfaces (Castrica et al., 2021; Jyoti & Bhattacharya, 2021). This time is long enough for the virus to survive on the surface of the package from production to delivery to the end consumer.

Although the packaging surfaces can be efficiently cleaned with disinfectant solutions such as ethanol (62–71%), hydrogen peroxide (0.5%), and sodium hypochlorite (0.1%) (Bedrosian et al., 2021), such treatment is not suitable for food contact materials due to safety concerns. No commercial products are safe for food contact applications and can instantly kill viruses, including SARS-CoV-2 (Priyadarshi et al., 2021a, 2021b). Therefore, there is a demand for developing food packaging materials or surface coating materials with antiviral potential that can kill/inactivate viruses through customized physicochemical and functional properties. However, much research has been done in the past few years on manufacturing biopolymer-based antimicrobial food packaging materials (Priyadarshi et al., 2021a, 2021b). Interestingly, most ingredients fabricating the packaging materials discussed in these studies possess adequate antiviral activity. Moreover, most of these materials are of natural origin, so they are environmentally friendly, biocompatible, and safe (Priyadarshi et al., 2022). However, there are few systematic studies on the antiviral potential of these materials directly or indirectly related to food packaging applications. Several extensive reports investigating the antiviral potential of these substances have also recently been published (Olaimat et al., 2020; Priyadarshi et al., 2022; Rakowska et al., 2021; Srisa et al., 2022). However, they do not present a detailed discussion of antiviral properties and mechanisms or focus entirely on applying these materials in antiviral food packaging. Additionally, the published reports mainly discuss antiviral development during the pandemic and do not provide a detailed perspective on post-COVID-19 opportunities (Mallakpour et al., 2021; Priyadarshi et al., 2022; Srisa et al., 2022).

This review systematically aims to classify and discuss these synthetic or natural antiviral substances. To this end, the infection mechanism of SARS-CoV-2 was briefly discussed. Further, the antiviral activity mechanism of the discussed materials, i.e., nanomaterials, biopolymers, natural oils, and plant extracts, was explained accordingly. The perspectives of these materials in developing antiviral food packaging were then presented while discussing recent advances in antiviral food packaging research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coronavirus structure - therapeutic targets for antiviral materials
ce:section.ce:para: Understanding the basic structure, life cycle, and infection mechanism of virus types, including SARS-CoV-2, is important for developing materials that can inhibit their replication and lifespan processes or cause structural degradation. The basic viral structure consists of an RNA or DNA core wrapped in a protein-based protective capsid (Rakowska et al., 2021). All viruses act as non-living entities outside a host organism and completely rely on the host-cell machinery to carry out their life processes (Brown, 2001). A virus's life cycle comprises attachment to the host cell, entry into the host cell, replication and assembly resulting in new virions, and finally, egress from the host cell (Brown, 2001). The viral replication in mammalian cells takes a few hours to complete, producing as many as 103 virions per cell (Rakowska et al., 2021). Each virus has its unique characteristic properties, such as infectivity, disinfectant susceptibility, and ability to replicate. The properties of viruses are highly dependent on their structure. For example, surface proteins in the viral capsid or envelope determine their attachment to host cells to initiate infection (Santos et al., 2020). Therefore, understanding the structure of a virus is important to understand its properties.

The capsid, the outer proteinaceous covering of the virus, is designed extremely explicitly and acts as a protective layer for the viral genome (Roos et al., 2007). Based on the capsid shape, the viruses are classified into four major categories: helical, icosahedral, spherical, and complex ( Fig. 1). Viruses such as influenza or coronavirus possess an additional protective lipid bilayer called an envelope (Rakowska et al., 2021; Sun & Whittaker, 2003). The envelope is not translated by the viral genome but is derived from the host cell's plasma membrane, where the viral replication and release occur through a budding process (Sun & Whittaker, 2003). This envelope is later integrated with additional viral proteins known as glycoproteins in the form of spikes (Bosch et al., 2003). The function of these spikes is to establish the virus-host interaction, facilitating the entry of the virus into the host cell (Santos et al., 2020). Besides, it also helps protect and mask the viral capsid proteins from the antibodies of the host’s immune system (Wisskirchen et al., 2014). This evasion of enveloped viruses from the host immune response is critical for understanding the development of viral infections. Despite the high ability of non-enveloped viruses to establish human-to-human transmission, most viral epidemics in recent years, including Ebola, measles, influenza, Zika, SARS, MERS, and Covid-19, have all been caused by enveloped viruses (Rakowska et al., 2021).

Coronavirus, SARS-CoV-2, is an enveloped single-stranded RNA virus belonging to Coronaviridae. Among the four genera of the Coronavirus family (i.e., α-CoV, β-CoV, γ-CoV, and δ-CoV), SARS-CoV-2 belongs to the β-CoV family (Wani et al., 2021). The general structure of coronavirus is shown in Fig. 2. The virus is spherical, and the genetic material of its central nucleus is covered with a protective capsid on the outside. The viral envelope exists just outside the capsid layer and is derived from the lipid bilayer of the host cell. The viral genome encodes four major proteins needed to carry out life processes (Roberts, Mahari, et al., 2021). Three of the four viral proteins, the spike glycoprotein (S), the matrix protein (M), and the envelope protein (E), are membrane proteins present on the surface, and the fourth, the nucleocapsid protein (N), fringes the genomic RNA (Roberts, Chouhan, et al., 2021; Santos et al., 2020). These proteins attract researchers as therapeutic targets for developing anti-coronavirus drugs. The most common target protein is the surface S-protein, which protrudes from the lipid envelope, giving the coronavirus its typical shape. The function of the S-protein is interaction with the host cell initiating its invasion (Priyadarshi et al., 2022). This protein is made up of two subunits, S1 and S2. The S1 subunit is the head of the spike and contains the receptor-binding domain (RBD) (Santos et al., 2020; Shahdeo, Roberts, et al., 2022). This domain recognizes and binds receptors on the surface of host cells to establish virus-host interactions. The RBD binds to the host cell's angiotensin-converting enzyme-2 (ACE-2) receptor (Priyadarshi et al., 2022; Shahdeo, Roberts et al., 2022). Once the host-virus interaction is established, the S2 subunit is activated. It forms a spike trunk, mediating the fusion of the viral envelope with the host cell membrane, consequently leading to the entry of the virus into the host cell, consequently leading to a series of events causing infection ( Fig. 3) (Mohamed et al., 2022). Therefore, antiviral substances block the virus's S-protein to suppress viral infection, preventing the interaction between the S-protein and the ACE-2 receptor on the host, thereby inhibiting the virus from initiating entry into the host cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Packaging materials - surface properties and virus persistence
ce:section.ce:para: The SARS-CoV-2 virus, like other viruses of the Coronavirus family, basically causes respiratory symptoms such as coughing and sneezing in humans. The intensity and severity of the symptoms depend on several factors, including the immunity of the infected individual. However, unlike other viruses of this family, SARS-CoV-2 has a very high transmission capability and spreads within the population at an exponential rate (Banerjee et al., 2020). Although airborne transmission is the primary route responsible for the spread of this virus, the viral droplets > 5 µm in size are known to lose their air-borne characteristics and remain stagnant on surfaces on which they fall (Klompas et al., 2020). These surfaces act as fomites, and their infectivity depends on their properties and characteristics (Rakowska et al., 2021). The packaging materials act as fomites and potential viral transmission carriers (Castrica et al., 2021). Since the surface plays a crucial role in viral persistence, understanding virus survival on different surfaces is important from the perspective of this review. A package's important surface properties that affect the adsorption and propagation of viruses are porosity, hydrophilic/hydrophobicity, and absorption properties. In addition, pH, temperature, humidity, exposure to light, roughness, ionic state, and other microorganisms on the surface are additional factors that control the persistence of viruses on specific surfaces (Rakowska et al., 2021).

The porosity of the surface plays an important role in virus survival, and most viruses persist longer on non-porous surfaces than on porous surfaces. Studies conducted on the surface viability of the influenza A virus have shown that it lasts longer (i.e., for 24–48 h) on non-porous surfaces such as stainless steel and plastic surfaces. On the other hand, the virus survived only 8–12 h on porous materials such as cloth and paper (Weber & Stilianakis, 2008). The poor persistence of viruses on porous surfaces is owed to the complete drying of viruses due to the absorption of viral droplets by porous surfaces. Besides, the insufficient elution of the virus particles from the porous surfaces is also believed to be another cause of its inactivation (Tiwari et al., 2006). Therefore, the absorption capacity of the surface is suggested as another factor affecting the persistence of the virus. Studies demonstrated that the survival of SARS-CoV was less than an hour on fluid-absorbing cotton-based personal protective equipment (PPE) hospital gowns (Lai et al., 2005). In contrast, the virus persisted on the fluid-repelling disposable gown for almost 24 h (Lai et al., 2005). Nevertheless, recent studies also reported contradictory results that SARS-CoV-1 and SARS-CoV-2 persist longer on fluid-absorbing surfaces such as cardboard than on non-absorbent materials such as stainless steel and plastics (Van Doremalen et al., 2020). The surface hydrophobicity is also an important factor that governs virus persistence. It has been reported that viruses with hydrophobic surface groups persist longer on hydrophobic surfaces, whereas viruses with hydrophilic surface groups persist longer on hydrophilic surfaces (Chattopadhyay et al., 2002). Therefore, determining capsids or envelope proteins on the viral surface can greatly aid antiviral surface design. This study once again supports the importance of understanding virus structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antiviral materials for food packaging applications
ce:section.ce:para: Chemical disinfectants are widely used to break the transmission chain of SARS-CoV-2, but their use has not been a long-term solution due to health risks and environmental concerns. They are also not recommended for use in food packaging for this reason. Therefore, developing packaging materials that can help prevent the spread of viruses by demonstrating antiviral activity appears sustainable and environmentally friendly. Food packaging with antiviral surfaces can be achieved using nanomaterials, biopolymers, and natural plant-derived ingredients.

Nanotechnology could play a key role by providing antiviral materials. Nanomaterials can be used as fillers or coatings to develop antiviral materials for everyday use, such as textiles, packaging, plastics, glass, etc. This approach potentially helps to achieve a virus-free environment. The scientific community has been exploring the potential of nanomaterials as antiviral agents for decades, but the advent of the COVID-19 pandemic has accelerated research and development in this area. Various nanomaterials have been reported to exhibit antiviral properties depending on their shape and size. The following sections detail the available antiviral nanomaterials and provide a brief perspective on their use in food packaging.

Various metal-based nanoparticles (e.g., copper, gold, silver, zinc, etc.) have been investigated for their antiviral effects. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have been extensively studied for their antiviral properties among metal nanoparticles. Recently, Morris et al. (2019) evaluated the antiviral and immunomodulatory effects of AgNPs. They showed the decreased replication of the Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) in vitro and in vivo. Moreover, a significant decrease in the expression of the various pro-inflammatory chemokines (i.e., CCL2, CCL3, CCL5) and pro-inflammatory cytokines (i.e., TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1α) was detected.

Previously, Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP)-coated AgNPs were evaluated for their antiviral activity against Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) (Lara et al., 2010). In this study, coated nanoparticles were homogenized in Replens gel and applied to a cervical tissue model. The results showed that it stopped the transmission of HIV in less than a minute and provided protection for 48 h. Cytotoxicity studies of PVP-coated AgNPs showed no adverse effects on explants. Similarly, Lv et al. (2014) reported that silver nanostructures of various shapes and sizes coated with PVP showed antiviral activity against the porcine coronavirus model. Another study involving curcumin-functionalized AgNPs showed notable antiviral activity against RSV infection (Yang et al., 2016). The curcumin functionalized AgNPs inactivated the virus by decreasing the adhesion of the virus structure, thus hindering the entry of the RSV to the host cell.

Various researchers also explored the antiviral properties of AgNPs-based nanocomposites. Chen et al. (2016) evaluated the antiviral activity of AgNPs/graphene-based nanocomposites on feline coronavirus (FCoV). They found that the high aspect ratio of graphene oxide and the antiviral potential of AgNPs yielded synergistic results in virucidal activity. Tuning of physicochemical properties was also possible in these nanocomposites by changing the composition. Moreover, the nanocomposites did not show cytotoxicity even at higher concentrations. In a recent study, sputtering silver nanoclusters/silica nanocomposites on an FFP3 mask revealed that the nanocomposite-coated mask could efficiently reduce the SARS-CoV-2 virus titer (Balagna et al., 2020). Wang et al. (2021) developed a filter using non-valent silver and TiO2 nanocomposite with chitosan, and they evaluated its potential for the inactivation and removal of air-borne MS2 bacteriophage particles. The developed filter could remove bacteriophage MS2 particles with about 93% efficiency. A recent study on the HCoV-229E virus involving AgNPs utilized reactive blade coating technology that could be useful for mass-producing protective gears (Abulikemu et al., 2022). First, the oleylamine-based reducing agent ink was applied to the surgical mask, and then, the silver precursor ink was applied to the reducing agent ink ( Fig. 4). The coated mask inactivated 99.98% HCoV-229E in about 30 min. The proposed coating method can efficiently produce antiviral surfaces on food packaging materials. Therefore, AgNPs-based antiviral materials have great potential and can be successfully used in antiviral food packaging and coating applications.

Various researchers have explored gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as antiviral agents. It is presumed that AuNPs bind to and block the viral receptors and, in turn, interfere with viral replication (Mikhailova, 2021). A possible mechanism behind the antiviral activity of AuNPs is probably their interaction with viral membrane proteins or ds-DNA (Lysenko et al., 2018). Meléndez-Villanueva et al. (2019) synthesized the AuNPs by green route using garlic extract. The antiviral potential of the as-synthesized AuNPs was evaluated against infection caused by the measles virus. This study showed that AuNP did not cause cytotoxicity in healthy cells and may help inhibit measles virus replication by blocking the viral receptor. In a similar study involving the Herpes simplex virus (HSV-1), El-Sheekh et al. (2022) found that AuNPs dose-dependently inhibited viral replication and inactivated 90% of HSV-1 at 31.25 μL. Dhanasezhian et al. (2019) synthesized AuNPs using Sargassum wightii extract and showed antiviral activity against two types of HSV (i.e., HSV-1 and HSV-2). Zacheo et al. (2020) evaluated the antiviral and virucidal effects of multi-sulfonated ligand-coated AuNPs against dengue virus (DENV). The viral assay showed that the linear long-chain mono-sulfonated ligand was less effective than the multi-sulfonated L2-AuNP. The results show that the number of sulfonate groups and their spatial arrangement significantly affect the binding of AuNPs to the viral envelope. Higher binding of L2-AuNPs to the envelope glycoprotein of DENV permanently inactivated the virus. Kim et al. (2020) suggested that AuNPs can break disulfide bonds in influenza viruses' Hemagglutinin (HA) protein. They synthesized porous AuNPs using a surfactant-free emulsion method and evaluated the proliferation of MDCK cells infected with various influenza virus strains (H1N1, H3N2, and H9N2). They demonstrated that porous AuNPs successfully attenuated the influenza virus and proliferated MDCK cells. Because coronaviruses have hemagglutinin glycoproteins, this approach seems likely, and the case of SARS-CoV-2 requires further investigation (Zandi et al., 2021).

Besides, zinc, copper, iron, and selenium-based nanoparticles have also been investigated for antiviral or virucidal activity, among other metal-based nanomaterials. Zinc is widely regarded as important for the development/repair/regeneration of several tissues due to its applicability as a component of signaling molecules and coenzymes in biological systems (Prasad, 2017). Zinc also helps boost an individual's immunity, as zinc supplements have been used effectively for several ailments, such as cardiovascular disease and diabetes (Read et al., 2019). Because zinc's immunological properties can increase antiviral resistance, it is believed that zinc supplementation may be an additional therapeutic approach for COVID-19 (Read et al., 2019; L. Zhang & Liu, 2020). Previous reports have also suggested zinc supplementation to enhance immunity against the H1N1 influenza virus (Sandstead & Prasad, 2010). Zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnONPs) are important metal oxide nanoparticles due to their significant antibacterial properties against many microorganisms such as bacteria, fungi, and viruses. Numerous scientists have investigated the antiviral properties of various micro- and nanostructured ZnO (Sarkar et al., 2022). The results have revealed that negatively charged ZnO micro- and nanostructures utilize an efficient virostatic trap mechanism to limit the entry of virions into host tissues (Sarkar et al., 2022). Farouk & Shebl (2018) investigated the antiviral potential of the surface-modified ZnONPs against the herpes simplex virus-I. They suggested its antiviral activity is due to the electrostatic interaction between virus structure and ZnONPs. Ghaffari et al. (2019) explored the antiviral potential of the ZnONPs and PEGylated ZnONPs against the H1N1 influenza virus. They showed that both ZnONPs and PEGylated ZnONPs neutralized the H1N1 influenza virus. Further, PEGylated ZnONPs had significantly reduced the virus titer compared to ZnONPs. Few studies also portrayed that Zn2+ ions have significantly repressed the replication of Nidovirus. Thus, increasing the Zn2+ ion concentration in the cells may hinder the replication of other RNA viruses, including SARS-CoV-2 (Gurunathan et al., 2020; Ishida, 2019). Melk et al. (2021) synthesized ZnONPs by the green synthesis route with Plumbago indica alcoholic extract, and the antiviral potential of the as-synthesized non-calcinated nanoparticles was evaluated. They found that the antiviral activity of the ZnONPs showed a reduction in the viral titer of HSV-1. Recently, El-Megharbel et al. (2021) developed a disinfectant nano-spray of ZnONPs, and its antiviral activity was evaluated against SARS-CoV-2. They showed that ZnONPs have effective antiviral activity at low concentrations (IC50 = 526 ng/mL). However, cytotoxicity results exhibited a cytotoxic effect on the host cell (CC50 = 292.2 ng/mL). The term CC50 stands for 50% cytotoxic concentration, defined as the nanoparticle concentration that can reduce cell viability by 50% compared to untreated controls. The IC50 stands for 50% inhibitory concentration, defined as the concentration of the nanoparticles capable of inhibiting 50% of viral replication compared to the virus control. Thus, it could be concluded that ZnONPs have antiviral activity, but they should be used with other biocompatible and/or bioactive components to reduce cytotoxicity. Nevertheless, ZnONPs have been safely used in food packaging materials (Priyadarshi, Kim, et al., 2021b; Priyadarshi & Negi, 2017), which can be evaluated for their antiviral action and potentially be considered in the form of antiviral food packaging.

In the case of copper-based micro-nano structures, Cu can initiate autophagy, and cells can use this mechanism to withstand the invasion of viruses. It has been found that Cu present in the host body initiates the formation of autophagic vacuoles. In addition, the toxicity due to Cu is very low due to the homeostatic mechanism. However, taking Cu-based supplements in large amounts (more than 10 mg per day) can cause serious side effects (Lönnerdal, 1996). In another aspect, Cu-based material surfaces can neutralize the virus in a shorter time than other materials, such as plastics and stainless steel (Van Doremalen et al., 2020). Previous studies have shown that brass with more than 70% copper neutralizes strain coronavirus 229E, and antiviral activity increases with copper concentration (Warnes et al., 2015). Yoshie et al. (2012) evaluated the antiviral activity of CuI nanoparticles against the H1N1 influenza virus, and they suggested that CuI aids in generating OH free radicals. These OH species neutralize the virus by degrading the viral proteins. In another study, Sportelli et al. (2020) confirmed that the free radicals enervated coronaviruses on copper-based surfaces.

The longevity of SARS-CoV-2 on solids may vary depending on the environmental conditions and the surface, where the stability varies on common solids and antimicrobial coatings (Hosseini, Behzadinasab, Benmamoun et al., 2021). However, the various mechanism of action for the inactivation of SARS-CoV-2 includes the generation of reactive oxygen species at the surface, contact-killing, adsorption, surface wettability, light-activated coatings, cold atmospheric plasma, ultraviolet light, ozone, heat treatment, etc. The development of active coatings on frequently contacted surfaces can inactivate SARS-CoV-2. Applying cupric oxide and zinc oxide-based antimicrobial coatings on surfaces, including railings and door handles, can reduce SARS-CoV-2 infection rapidly (Hosseini, Behzadinasab, Chin et al., 2021; Hosseini, Chin et al., 2021). Silver oxide coatings can also be used as Anti- SARS-CoV-2 coatings for touch screens (Hosseini et al., 2022).

The iron oxide nanoparticles (IO-NPs) have magnetic properties and can be readily modified/surface functionalized. Bromberg et al. (2012) synthesized poly hexamethylene biguanide functionalized core-shell silica-iron oxide magnetic nanoparticles. The results of antiviral activity studies against various virus strains showed a significant virucidal effect of nanoparticles. A possible cause of this virucidal activity could be the amino-alkylation of the viral genetic material. This amino-alkylation process inactivates the virus by interfering with its DNA/RNA replication. Since iron-based substances can induce homeostatic dysregulation in patients with SARS-CoV-2 infection, further studies on IO-NP are needed (Bisso & Leroux, 2020; Cardoso et al., 2020). In another study, molecular docking of IO-NPs was performed to investigate their interaction with SARS-CoV-2 (Abo-zeid et al., 2020). The results suggested hydrogen bonding between IO-NP and the S-protein receptor-binding domain (RBD) of SARS-CoV-2.

Furthermore, hydrophobic interactions of IO-NPs were detected with Phe497, Ser494, and Leu455 at the active site of the S-protein receptor binding domain ( Fig. 5). The binding of IO-NPs could reduce the virion attachment to the host cells (Abo-zeid et al., 2020). Another study involving IO-NPs demonstrated the antiviral activity against the H1N1 influenza virus. Small-sized IO-NPs (10–15 nm) were synthesized. The possible cause behind the antiviral activity was suggested to be blocking the virus's active site by IO-NPs, which can bind to the virus and inhibit its absorption (Kumar et al., 2019).

Various researchers have explored the antiviral potential of selenium-based nanostructures. An organic selenium species, Ebselen, is being studied because it can neutralize viruses by forming covalent bonds with the SARS-CoV-2 virion cell membrane (Jin et al., 2020). In another study, Ebselen at a concentration of 10 μM demonstrated an inhibitory effect on Vero cells infected with SARS-CoV-2 and was considered safe for use in humans. Ebselen also helped reduce/prevent liver damage caused by various substances, microbes, and the immune system (Kono et al., 2001). The main problem with selenium is toxicity at high doses. However, nano selenium is less cytotoxic than other selenium-based compounds and therefore has been recommended to treat numerous diseases, such as Huntington's disease and cancer (Cong et al., 2019; He et al., 2021).

Graphene and graphene-based nanomaterials have a vast history of being used as functional materials in various applications, including biological applications such as antibacterial, wound healing, drug or gene delivery, tissue engineering, cancer treatment, and more (Ebrahimi et al., 2022; Y. Yang et al., 2013). The properties of graphene that make it suitable for these applications are its controllable biocompatibility, easy functionalization, and antimicrobial activity. Furthermore, the dominant van der Walls forces between the graphene nanosheets allow them to be readily incorporated into polymers to form functional nanocomposites that can be used for various purposes, including food packaging (Ebrahimi et al., 2022). Graphene-based nanomaterials are largely classified into pure graphene, graphene oxide (GO), and reduced graphene oxide (rGO). Pure graphene is hydrophobic, limiting its biological applications, but GO is a hydrophilic material due to oxygen-containing functional groups such as -COOH, CO, C-O-H, -C-O-C-, and so on, and can be widely applied in this field of research (Deepachitra et al., 2015; Ebrahimi et al., 2022; Purohit et al., 2019, 2020). In addition, rGO exhibits much better dispersion in aqueous biological media and better electrical and photocatalytic properties. The antibacterial/antiviral action of graphene-based materials occurs through various mechanisms: (i) physical interaction between the sharp edges of graphene-based structures with microbial cell membrane or viral capsid (Akhavan & Ghaderi, 2010; Ye et al., 2015), (ii) production of ROS (Musico et al., 2014), (iii) electrostatic interaction of the positively charged moieties on microbial cell or viral capsid/envelope with the negative charge on graphene sheets (Ye et al., 2015), and (iv) microbial/viral surface protein inactivation due to wrapping by graphene sheets, resulting in the formation of aggregates (Akhavan et al., 2011; Bhatia et al., 2021). Due to their strong antibacterial action, graphene-based materials are potential candidates for manufacturing antiviral packaging materials. Several studies have reported the antiviral efficacy of graphene-based materials, especially GO, against several human infectious viruses, including SARS-CoV-2. GO exhibits antiviral activity against HIV by inhibiting the viral protein R (Vpr), a regulatory protein of the virus (Zhang et al., 2013). A subunit of this protein, Vpr13–33, plays an important role in regulating the HIV nuclear protein importation. GO interacts with Vpr13–33 to form aggregates, resulting in morphological changes and consequently inactivating HIV function. Ebola is a type of virus for which GO is very active. The Ebola virus gene is translated into protein VP40 to form end-to-end connections, forming filaments of the viral substrate. Several studies, including molecular dynamics (MD) simulations and graphene pelleting analysis, have demonstrated the ability of graphene sheets to interact with VP40 protein to cause viral substrate damage (GC et al., 2017). The simulation results demonstrated the formation of various graphene-VP40 complexes indicating significant interaction between the two.

The results were experimentally confirmed via graphene pelleting assay, where the pelleting of VP40 occurred in a solution of GO with concentrated VP40 dimers. The pelleting of VP40 increased with GO concentration and signified substantial GO affinity for VP40. GO also showed significant inhibition of SARS-CoV-2 infection, as in-vitro and in-silico studies demonstrated. The in-vitro studies on the infection of Vero cells by SARS-CoV-2 demonstrated a noteworthy reduction when the virus particles were pretreated with GO (De Maio et al., 2021). Also, the graphene or GO-loaded polyurethane (PU) and cotton fabrics demonstrated almost complete viral inhibition potential when SARS-CoV-2-containing media were either cultured with or filtered through them ( Fig. 6). In-silico studies using molecular dynamics simulations and molecular docking were also performed to determine the mechanism of SARS-CoV-2 inhibition by graphene (Raval et al., 2020). The results indicated an increase in the free binding energy of graphene towards the virus particles with an increase in the number of layers. In a different study, molecular docking was used to determine the interaction of GO with SARS-CoV-2 spike proteins (both open and closed states) (Unal et al., 2021). In both states, the S-protein demonstrated high interaction with GO sheets. The study indicated a significant reduction of viral infection on exposure to biological-grade nanoscale GO sheets with a few hundred nanometers lateral dimensions. The GO sheets did not destroy the viral particles but blocked the surface spike protein, reducing infection. Nevertheless, this study has shown the tremendous potential of graphene-based nanostructures to neutralize a wide range of viral infections, particularly SARS-CoV-2, which represents the potential for manufacturing antiviral materials for food packaging.

Silicon-based nanomaterials are more biocompatible and less cytotoxic than other commonly used metal-based nanoparticles such as silver, copper, gold, etc. (Chen & Liang, 2020). Silica is not known to have high antimicrobial properties, but several structural and functional modifications can confer appropriate antimicrobial activity (Chen & Liang, 2020). These modified silicon-based nanostructures have also been studied for their antiviral effects against several human infectious viruses, including SARS-CoV-2. Functionalized silicon nanoparticles (solid and mesoporous) mimicking glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) were prepared and studied for their ability to inhibit Herpes simplex virus (HSV-1 and HSV-2) infections of the host cell (Lee et al., 2016). HSV attaches to the host cell and initiates entry into it. This attachment occurs through interactions between viral surface glycoproteins and cell surface proteins. GAG-mimicking silicon nanoparticles competitively bind to viral surface proteins, blocking them and preventing their attachment to host cells. Thus, the entry of the virus into the host cell is blocked, and HSV infection is suppressed. The antiviral activity of porous silicon nanoparticles was also studied against HIV and respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) (Osminkina et al., 2014). The particles efficiently reduced the viral infection, even at a concentration below 0.1 mg/mL, significantly lower than the cytotoxic concentration. The results indicated a non-specific interaction between the virus particles and the silicon nanoparticles, attributed to their porous structure facilitating its binding to viral surface proteins. The porous structure provides the surface with abundant functional groups that readily bind non-specifically to the viral surface proteins (De Souza E Silva et al., 2016; Osminkina et al., 2014). The studies present silicon nanoparticles as a broad-spectrum prophylactic against viral infections (De Souza E Silva et al., 2016). The porous silicon nanoparticles can be potentially used as functional antiviral nanofillers in food packaging polymers.

The antiviral potential of various quantum dots has not been extensively investigated. Numerous research groups have recently begun exploring quantum dots for various applications as antiviral agents (Chen & Liang, 2020). The antiviral activity of cadmium telluride quantum dots coated with glutathione was reported, and the antiviral activity against the pseudorabies virus was evaluated (Du et al., 2015). Cadmium telluride quantum dots are proposed to interact with viral surface proteins and prevent the pseudorabies virus from attaching to host cells. Moreover, Cd2+ released from cadmium telluride quantum dots significantly reduced the number of viruses infecting the host cell. The size and surface charge of the synthesized cadmium telluride quantum dots (QDs) greatly influenced the antiviral properties, as positively charged QDs had higher antiviral activity than negatively charged QDs (Sarkar et al., 2022). Another research work demonstrated the antiviral mechanism of the less cytotoxic quantum dots, i.e., silver sulfide nanoclusters. Du et al. (2018) developed the nanoclusters of Ag2S and evaluated their virucidal effect for the first time against a coronavirus model of porcine epidemic diarrhea virus (PEDV). Investigation of virus titer showed that nanoclusters could significantly reduce infection by inactivating the virus. They also postulated an antiviral mechanism, delineating that Ag2S nanoclusters could inhibit viral budding and the synthesis of viral negative-strand RNA.

The carbon dots have been found to exhibit notable sterilization efficiency. However, the antiviral activity of carbon dots is highly dependent on the synthesis conditions and carbon precursors. Therefore, an in-depth analysis of the antiviral mechanisms of various carbon dots should be performed (Chen & Liang, 2020). Du et al. (2016) showed that carbon dots could neutralize RNA and DNA viruses in their research. The antiviral activity of the carbon dots was evaluated against the porcine reproductive and respiratory syndrome virus and pseudorabies virus. Several carbon dots have been studied to treat human coronavirus HCoV-229E infection. These nanoscale materials were derived from citric acid/ethylenediamine and modified boronic acid ligands. Quantum dots synthesized using these methods effectively blocked and neutralized HCoV-229E entry in a concentration-dependent manner. The interaction between HCoV-229E entry receptors and the functional groups present on the synthesized carbon quantum dots directed the inhibition of viral replication (Tavakol et al., 2021).

Similarly, carbon dots derived from benzoxazine monomer were prudent in hindering various infections such as adenoviruses, flaviviruses, porcine parvovirus, and non-enveloped viruses (Huang et al., 2019). Likewise, amine or boronic acid surface-functionalized carbon dots exhibited antiviral activity against HSV-1 by deterring the attachment of the virus to the host cells (Barras et al., 2016). In another study, curcumin functionalized cationic carbon dots were synthesized by a one-step method, and their antiviral efficacy was tested against PEDV in a coronavirus model (Ting et al., 2018). The results showed that curcumin-functionalized cationic carbon dots interfered with the proliferation of PEDV with superior antiviral activity than the neat carbon dots. Several other herbal components with medicinal properties, such as glycyrrhizic acid, have promoted the development of biocompatible carbon quantum dots. Glycyrrhizic acid-based carbon dots significantly reduced viral titers of porcine reproductive and respiratory syndrome viruses (Tong et al., 2020). These quantum dots suppress intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) accumulation and simultaneously induce immune responses against the porcine reproductive and respiratory syndrome virus. In addition, the glycyrrhizin acid-based carbon dots showed high antiviral activity against PEDV and pseudorabies virus (Tong et al., 2020). Sarkar et al. (2022) fabricated quantum dots with smaller sizes (1–10 nm) and different shapes capable of penetrating the SARS-CoV-2 virus structure more easily. The S protein of SARS-CoV-2 can be immobilized using the positive surface charge of carbon-based quantum dots (Ting et al., 2018). Dong et al. (2017) showed that quantum dots with net positive charges on their surface could interact with the negative RNA strand of the SARS-CoV-2 virus and produce reactive oxygen species, inactivating the SARS-CoV-2. Other researchers suggested that carbon dots can activate the interferon-stimulated genes, inhibiting viral replication, specifically interferon-alpha development (Manivannan & Ponnuchamy, 2020). Correspondingly, inserting specific functional groups on quantum dots aids in their effective interaction with entry receptors of the SARS-CoV-2, hindering virus replication (Iannazzo et al., 2018). It was demonstrated that the functional groups of carbon dots interact with human coronavirus-229E's S protein, therefore eliminating the virus's entry to the host cell by reducing the attachment efficiency of the virus to the host cell membrane. Hence, there is a need to perform more comprehensive research and optimization on developing quantum dots to target COVID-19. In this regard, developing new methods and functionalization approaches (including functional molecules) will soon find novel nanostructures for COVID-19 therapeutics to defeat this deadly disease (Manivannan & Ponnuchamy, 2020).

Polymer materials are widely used to develop various packaging materials, such as active and smart packaging. Packaging systems must improve food safety and extend shelf life by protecting and maintaining quality. Polymer materials have beneficial characteristics such as easy processability, abundance, and surface chemistry, making them suitable for developing antiviral active packaging materials. Polymers can also be easily modified strategically, including chemical modification, physical modification, mixing, and integration with active substances. In addition, biopolymer materials are attracting attention as food packaging materials due to their biodegradability and excellent sustained-release properties, making them an advantageous option for use as a base material combined with various active ingredients (Fabra et al., 2018; Priyadarshi & Rhim, 2020). Various biopolymers with nanomaterials, essential oils, and plant extracts have been used to make antiviral packaging films or coatings. These studies demonstrate the utilization of biopolymers as carrier materials for active ingredients with antiviral activity (Randazzo et al., 2018). The active ingredient is trapped in the polymeric material and can be released depending on storage conditions such as relative humidity, temperature, and pH.

In addition to being utilized as carrier matrices for active antiviral components, several biopolymers possess characteristic antiviral properties. Although various biopolymers have been used for food packaging applications, polysaccharides are the most prominent antiviral biopolymers ( Table 1). Some important ones are heparin, dextran sulfate, sulfoevenan, hyaluronic acid, cellulose sulfate, carrageenan, chondroitin polysulfate, chitosan, etc. These polymers displayed decent antiviral activity (Bianculli et al., 2020; Randazzo et al., 2018).

Polyanionic biopolymers such as sulfated polysaccharides, including dextran sulfate, heparin, and agar, exhibit antiviral activity (Bianculli et al., 2020). The mechanism by which polyanions exert antiviral effects is adsorption inhibition. They block cell adhesion of virions by adsorbing to the virus's surface and preventing interaction with host cells ( Fig. 7). However, this virion-biopolymer interaction is also reversible and inefficient. Another reported mechanism involves interferon production by host cells when exposed to anionic polysaccharides (Merigan & Finkelstein, 1968). Interferons are signaling proteins released by a virus-infected cell to warn nearby cells so that they can prepare and defend themselves against possible viral infection. It has been clinically confirmed that sulfated polysaccharides, dextran sulfate, and heparin are strong HIV inhibitors (Ito et al., 1987). In addition, most polysaccharides obtained from seaweeds, such as carrageenans, fucans, alginates, galactans, and sea algae extract, are known to have potent broad-spectrum antiviral activity (Bianculli et al., 2020). It has also been reported that the antiviral activity of the sulfated polysaccharides depends on their degree of sulfation (DS). DS refers to the number of sulfate groups per monosaccharide unit. λ-Carrageenan (DS = 35%) was observed to have a 10-fold higher antiviral efficacy against HIV compared to κ-carrageenan (DS = 25%) (Witvrouw & De Clercq, 1997). Other non-sulfated polyanions like polyhydroxycarboxylates also possess antiviral activity (Bianculli et al., 2020; Merigan & Finkelstein, 1968).

In addition to polyanionic biopolymers, biopolymers having polycationic properties, such as chitosan, polylysine, polyarginine, and cationic dextran derivatives, have also been reported to have antiviral properties. The antiviral mechanism of action of polycationic biopolymers, such as chitosan, involves electrostatic interaction and destabilization with the viral capsid. It can also inhibit virus-host cell interactions by binding to negatively charged domains on the virus surface (Bianculli et al., 2020; Mallakpour et al., 2021). Chitosan has been used for the inhibition of norovirus. However, despite its insufficient effectiveness, some methods have been reported to induce antiviral effects. Higher concentrations, longer incubation times, higher levels of deacetylation, and higher molecular weight may enhance the antiviral activity of chitosan (Bianculli et al., 2020; Mallakpour et al., 2021).

Therefore, several biopolymers widely studied in food packaging applications possess innate antiviral activity that was not explored until recently. As discussed in the introduction, the first signs of packaging-based cross-contamination from the SARS-CoV-2 virus emerged from animal product packaging. There have been several studies where ionic polysaccharides, such as alginate (Priyadarshi, Kim et al., 2021a), agar (Priyadarshi et al., 2021a, 2021b), carrageenan (Saedi et al., 2021), chitosan (Roy et al., 2021), etc., have been suggested for the packaging of meat products. It can be logically assumed that these biodegradable food packaging materials are antiviral and can protect packaged food from viral infection. Therefore, biodegradable polymeric materials can be used as antiviral packaging, especially based on ionic polysaccharides.

Traditional medicine is based on and derived from medicinal plant species in almost all human civilizations worldwide. Since ancient times, medicinal plants have been used to treat many diseases and ailments (Mukhtar et al., 2008). Nearly 25% of modern medicines are also estimated to contain compounds isolated from plants (Mukhtar et al., 2008). The medicinal properties of the plant are attributed to its essential oils and extracts, which possess various secondary metabolites such as terpenoids, alkaloids, flavonoids, etc. (Sytar et al., 2021). These metabolites possess excellent bactericidal and insecticidal properties and are widely used in the food and pharmaceutical industries (Mukhtar et al., 2008). Besides, in the past decade, much focus has been given to the antiviral activity of the metabolites present in medicinal plant extracts and essential oils (Ma & Yao, 2020; Sytar et al., 2021). The antiviral potential of such herbal metabolites has been reported against several infectious viral strains in the human population, including those of the coronavirus family, especially SARS-CoV-2 (Sytar et al., 2021). This section describes common medicinal plants with extracts and essential oils with an antiviral activity that are already in use or can be potentially used as antiviral ingredients when developing active food packaging materials.

As the number of infections and deaths from the SARS-CoV-2 virus worldwide has grown exponentially, researchers have focused on several approved preclinical antiviral drugs, especially natural substances used to treat infections, since ancient times (Sytar et al., 2021). Plant essential oils were among several such substances, and their chemical constituents were proposed as potential agents for treating viral infections. Due to the lipophilic nature of essential oils, they are known to penetrate and destroy the viral membrane easily (Sun & Whittaker, 2003). In addition, phytochemicals in essential oils interfere with viral replication and assembly processes, thereby reducing transmission (Ma & Yao, 2020; Wani et al., 2021). Essential oils are also known to positively improve the host respiratory system because they help dissolve mucus and dilate the bronchi (Wani et al., 2021). Although much literature is available on the antiviral effects of plant essential oils against various virus types, the data on antiviral activity against SARS-CoV are limited and are based on in-vitro and in-silico studies (Wani et al., 2021).

Garlic oil has a long history of treating influenza, cough, and other respiratory infections. Molecular docking studies have revealed that 17 active compounds in garlic oil have strong interactions with the key protease (Mpro), which aids in viral replication, and the ACE-2 surface protein, which aids in viral entry into host cells (Thuy et al., 2020). These active ingredients bind to these important proteins in the structure of the virus and block their function, thereby inactivating the virus. The in-silico studies were also conducted on the interaction between the S1 subunit (receptor binding domain) of the S-protein of SARS-CoV-2 and various essential oils derived from plants of different families such as Lamiaceae, Lauraceae, Myrtaceae, Apiaceae, Geraniaceae, and Fabaceae. Essential oils include monoterpenes, terpenoid phenols, and phenylpropanoids such as anethole, cinnamaldehyde, carvacrol, geraniol, cinnamyl acetate, L-4-terpineol, thymol, pulegone, etc. As revealed in molecular docking studies, these components effectively inhibit the viral S-protein (Kulkarni et al., 2020). Eucalyptus globulus essential oil has been traditionally used as a therapeutic agent for several respiratory ailments (Parham et al., 2020). In addition, the strong antiviral activity of Eucalyptus oil was reported against Herpes simplex virus strains (HSV-1 and HSV-2) (Schnitzler et al., 2001), which prompted the researchers to study its effect on SARS-CoV-2. The in vitro assays and in-silico studies on Jensenone, the active constituent in Eucalyptus oil, showed its reaction with Mpro of SARS-CoV-2 via hydrophobic, ionic, and H-bond interactions. This reaction resulted in the Jansenone/Mpro complex and the inactivation of the viral proteins (Sharma & Kaur, 2020a). Also, 1,8-cineole, commonly known as eucalyptol, is another active component of Eucalyptus oil that exhibits efficient binding with Mpro, inhibiting viral replication (Sharma & Kaur, 2020b). In another study, three common constituents of various essential oils, i.e., carvacrol, menthol, and eugenol, were reported to exhibit high affinities towards important viral proteins such as S-protein, Mpro, and RdRp (Asif et al., 2020; A. Kumar et al., 2020). Essential oils from lemon balm (Melissia officinalis) also comprise phytoconstituents such as luteolin-7-glucoside-3′-glucuronide, melitric acid-A, and quadranoside-III, which have displayed strong binding affinities with Mpro and S-protein of SARS-CoV-2 (Prasanth et al., 2021). The study indicated the potential anti-coronavirus properties of lemon balm essential oil (Patel et al., 2021; Prasanth et al., 2021). Another less commonly targeted viral protein is the SARS-CoV-2 papain-like protease (PLpro), essential in viral replication (Goswami et al., 2020). An in-silico study determined the binding affinities between compounds found in rhizomes galangal, ginger, and curcuma, with PLpro. The studies revealed the presence of eight compounds in galangal and ginger that bind with high affinity to closed PLpro conformer and hence have potential as SARS-CoV-2 PLpro inhibitors (Goswami et al., 2020; Parham et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Natural medicinal substances against other similar human infecting viruses
ce:section.ce:para: The antiviral efficacy of natural oils and their constituent compounds has been studied for a long time. In-vitro methods such as plaque reduction assay, cytopathic effect reduction, and viral yield reduction assay are commonly employed to estimate antiviral activity (Ma & Yao, 2020). Also, antiviral components' viral inhibition potential is dose-dependent and estimated in terms of IC50, i.e., the concentration of antiviral agents required to reduce the viral infection by 50% (Ma & Yao, 2020). Some commonly used essential oils, such as tea tree, oregano, star anise, apple mint, and eucalyptus, have been effective against herpes simplex virus HSV-1, an enveloped virus such as SARS-CoV (Ma & Yao, 2020). Among them, the antiviral activity of star anise essential oil was the strongest, with an IC50 value of 1 μg/mL against this virus strain (Astani et al., 2011). In addition, essential oils from various medicinal plants from families such as Labiatae, Umbelliferae, and Myrtaceae have also been reported to exhibit significant antiviral effects against HSV-1 (Ma & Yao, 2020; Orhan et al., 2012). Among the components derived from essential oils, the most effective was β-caryophyllene, exhibiting an IC50 value of 0.25 μg/mL (Ma & Yao, 2020). The study indicated that essential oils from β-caryophyllene-containing plants such as cloves, oregano, cinnamon, black pepper, cannabis, etc. (Francomano et al., 2019) might be effective anti-viral agents against not only HSV but also SARS-CoV. Influenza is another enveloped RNA virus similar to SARS-CoV (Rakowska et al., 2021). Many reports indicated the high anti-influenza activity of essential oils obtained from anise, clary sage, marjoram, cinnamon, thyme, and bergamot (Choi, 2018; Vimalanathan & Hudson, 2014). In addition to essential oils, studies of extracted compounds were conducted to determine their efficacy against the influenza virus. Germacrone is derived from Curcumae rhizoma and is reported to be a broad-spectrum antiviral compound due to its ability to act at multiple stages of viral replication process (Wu et al., 2016). Other components such as β-santalol and terpinen-4-ol are also reported to be efficient anti-influenza agents (Garozzo et al., 2011; Paulpandi et al., 2012).

In addition to HSV and influenza, several essential oils have been studied for efficacy against other virus types. Osmunda regalis essential oil also exhibited high antiviral activity against the Coxsackie virus with an IC50 value of 2.24 μg/mL (Bouazzi et al., 2018). Essential oils from Eucalyptus bicostata and Dysphania ambrosioides showed efficient anti-Coxsackie activities (Elaissi et al., 2012; Mokni et al., 2019). The essential oregano oil exhibited strong inhibition of human rotavirus and murine norovirus due to carvacrol, which acts as the active component. Very potent inhibition of HIV strains was reported by essential oils extracted from Thymus vulgaris, Rosmarinus officinalis, and Cymbopogon citratus, with IC50 values ranging from 0.05 μg/mL to 0.83 μg/mL (Feriotto et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of antiviral action of essential oils
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, most essential oils exhibit higher antiviral activity against enveloped viruses than those without envelopes. The reason is the lipid-based structure of the viral envelope derived from the membrane lipid bilayer of the host cell. This makes the virus envelope lipophilic, and the essential oils can easily interact (Wani et al., 2021). On the other hand, essential oils cannot readily interact with non-enveloped viruses with fewer target sites. The Time-of-Addition experiment reported and confirmed the interaction between the essential oil and the viral envelope structure (Ma & Yao, 2020). The essential oils can also mask the viral surface proteins that play an essential role in adsorption and entry into the host cell. Another mechanism by which essential oils exert antiviral effects is the change in viral morphology studied by TEM (Gilling et al., 2014). Virus capsids swell and break down when exposed to essential oils, making them inactive.

Protein inhibition is another mechanism by which essential oils inactivate viruses (Ma & Yao, 2020). Cedar leaf essential oil exerts a strong inhibitory effect on the hemagglutinin protein of the influenza virus, an important membrane protein involved in the entry of the virus into the host cell (Vimalanathan & Hudson, 2014). Essential oils from Thymus vulgaris, Rosmarinus officinalis, and Cymbopogon citratus interact with proteins essential for HIV transcription. This interaction blocks its multiplication within the host cell (Feriotto et al., 2018). In some cases, applying essential oils changes the endosomal or lysosomal pH, which is crucial during the viral infection. For instance, tea tree essential oil has been reported to interrupt the acidification of the intralysosomal compartment, which is essential for uncoating the influenza virus during the replication process (Garozzo et al., 2011).

Nevertheless, the interaction and blocking of the viral surface proteins or envelope proteins are still presumed to be the most common mechanism responsible for the antiviral action of essential oils (Ma & Yao, 2020). The mechanism also depends on the type of essential oil component, the target virus, and the structure of the target virus.

After the global turmoil caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, interest in food packaging research for developing antiviral food packaging materials is rapidly increasing. Although few studies have been conducted on the antiviral properties of food packaging materials, some interesting studies create a roadmap for other researchers to explore this area further. Some important studies on antiviral food packaging development are summarized in Table 2. Research and development in food packaging have given rise to a surge aimed at the safe delivery of food to the end consumer in the present situation (Priyadarshi et al., 2021a, 2021b, 2022). Among the available technologies, active packaging can be manufactured by incorporating active agents as additives in the packaging materials or used as an exterior or interior coating for certain packaging materials (Mudenur et al., 2021; Priyadarshi et al., 2021a, 2021b, 2022). Active packaging materials are currently available in biodegradable active packaging, active edible films, coatings, multi-layer packaging, etc.

Based on the discussion, effective packaging can be obtained by developing internal and external coating to protect food and provide antiviral properties. The external coating on polyethylene based on methyl hydroxypropyl cellulose, ZnO nanoparticles, geraniol, and carvacrol delivers antiviral and antibacterial properties (Mizielińska et al., 2021). The fabrication of active layers based on methyl hydroxypropyl cellulose containing CO2 extracts of raspberry seeds, pomegranate seeds, and rosemary on packaging films can extend the shelf life of food products (Ordon, Nawrotek et al., 2021). Applying these coating materials to polyethylene can increase the freshness and quality of food. Developing biodegradable multilayer films with antiviral activity could also help maintain food safety and quality. Incorporating an active interlayer of zein and cinnamaldehyde in PHB-based packaging materials has been studied (Fabra et al., 2016). The multilayer packaging system has been tested against Murine norovirus, feline calicivirus, and hepatitis A virus, where feline calicivirus can be completely inactivated by incorporating cinnamaldehyde (2.60 mg/cm2) in the biodegradable multilayer system. Also, the electrospun coating based on poly(3-hydroxybutyrate-co-3-hydroxyvalerate) and silver nanoparticles was used for antiviral surfaces of packaging (Castro-Mayorga et al., 2017). Silver nanoparticles have received considerable interest in food packaging applications for their potent broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity.

In addition, food can be a carrier for virus transmission, so edible antiviral coatings could be used as a new strategy to provide food safety. An edible coating is a type of food packaging where a thin layer of edible materials is applied to food products using dipping, spraying, brushing, and other methods (Ghosh et al., 2019, 2021). On the other hand, edible film is another material used for food packaging with edible and biodegradable properties (Ghosh et al., 2022; Ghosh & Katiyar, 2022). The materials for manufacturing edible coatings and films are polysaccharides, proteins, lipids, and additives. It should be noted that edible films and coatings are good carriers for functional substances. Food additives such as plant extracts, essential oils, and other active ingredients are widely used to manufacture antiviral coating materials. The edible antiviral coating based on κ-, ι-, and λ-carrageenan and green tea extract has been tested against murine Norovirus and Hepatitis A virus (Falcó, Randazzo et al., 2019). Adding green tea extract to carrageenan enhanced the antiviral activity of edible coatings to improve the safety of raspberries and blueberries at ambient and refrigerated temperatures. The edible coatings and films based on chitosan and grape seed extract delivered antiviral activity, in which 1.75 and 3.58 log PFU/mL (PFU: plaque forming units) reductions after 3 h were obtained from 107 PFU/mL of initial count by 1.0% and 2.5% grape seed extract aqueous solution, respectively (Amankwaah et al., 2020).

The edible films containing alginate, lipids, and natural extracts (green tea extract and grape seed extract) exhibited potent antiviral and antioxidant properties (Fabra et al., 2018). Grape seed extract and green tea extract in developing biopolymer-based edible films showed antiviral activity against hepatitis A virus and murine Norovirus, where ∼2 log reduction was observed for the films containing 0.75 g extract/g alginate. Blueberries can develop an edible antiviral coating using Persian gum, gelatin, and allyl isothiocyanate. The addition of allyl isothiocyanate significantly increased the activity compared to the neat Persian gum (Sharif et al., 2021).

First detected in Wuhan city, China, in December 2019, the coronavirus (SARS-CoV-2) disease spread worldwide and eventually severely disrupted the global economy as several countries implemented lockdowns to prevent the exponential spread of this strain of the virus. However, the delivery services for essential commodities, such as food, were still in place, leading to reports mentioning the role of packaging materials as fomites. Fomite-based virus transmission was more prevalent in food packaging since every food product uses packaging composed of plastics and papers on which SARS-CoV-2 persisted for a long time.

To solve the problems in the food packaging field, there is an urgent need to develop packaging materials having antiviral property that inactivates or destroys the virus immediately. Interestingly, studies on active packaging conducted over the past decade to prevent microbial spoilage of foods and extend shelf life provide several already in-use ingredients with antiviral activity. These antiviral substances include metallic/non-metallic nanostructures, certain biopolymers such as sulfated and ionic polysaccharides, several extracts of medicinal plants, and essential oils. Although the antiviral application of manufactured packaging has not been explored in depth, many reports of the antiviral activity of these individual materials have been reported.

Several biopolymers, such as alginates, carrageenan, and chitosan, commonly used to make biodegradable food packaging films, have been reported to have antiviral activity. Packaging materials made from antiviral biopolymers or biopolymer-coating on the surface prevent virus particles from persisting, preventing cross-contamination. In addition, incorporating sustainable additives into these polymer films can enhance the antiviral potential of these films. Antiviral additives such as nanomaterials, natural oils, and herbal extracts help promote the physicochemical properties of packaging materials while contributing to their antiviral efficacy. From a sustainability point of view, biopolymer films combined with natural oils and plant extracts could be a completely natural, economical, safe, and environmentally friendly option for manufacturing biopolymer-based antiviral packaging. Biodegradable polymeric materials combined with natural oils and plant extracts have long been explored for potential food packaging applications. Although these substances have been widely studied for several functions, such as antibacterial, antifungal, and antioxidant properties, few reports show antiviral food packaging properties. Exploring the potential of natural antiviral bioactive ingredients in food packaging is essential to identify future cases of viral cross-contamination and ensure food safety and sustainability. In addition, it is estimated that the demand for biodegradable antiviral food packaging and coating materials will further increase during the post-pandemic period, and an analysis of the practicality and efficient and rapid commercialization of these natural antiviral materials is required.
